1: David is told that the Philistines are fighting against Keilah and raiding the threshing floors. Keilah was a fortified town in the Shephelah southwest of Adullam, in Judahite territory. Raiding threshing floors was a classic harvest-time tactic — grain was the agricultural wealth of the region and threshing floors were exposed sites outside the city walls. David is being drawn into a role as protector of his own tribe.
2: David inquires of YHWH: 'Shall I go and strike these Philistines?' YHWH says: 'Go, strike the Philistines and save Keilah.' The brief oracle-consultation is technically an ephod inquiry (v6) — the binary yes/no form of the ûrîm wəṯummîm. This is the first of several oracle consultations in ch 23, establishing a pattern: David consults before acting. The contrast with Saul, who acts without consulting (and is later condemned for it in 28:6), is the narrator's quiet theological commentary.
3: David's men express their fear: 'We are afraid here in Judah; how much more if we go to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?' The men's reluctance is understandable — they are already hunted fugitives, not a standing army. Their hesitation leads David to consult YHWH a second time (v4), establishing that divine confirmation, not human courage, is the basis for military action.
4: David inquires of YHWH again. YHWH answers: 'Rise and go down to Keilah, for I will give the Philistines into your hand.' The double oracle confirms the action against the second-guessing of the men. The repetition of consultation is not lack of faith but the narrative's insistence that David acts only on divine authorization. Where Saul acts precipitously (13:9-10) or consults the dead (28:7), David repeatedly consults the living God.
5: David and his men go to Keilah, fight the Philistines, and strike them with a great blow — also driving off their livestock. The military victory is total and immediate, confirming the oracle. The livestock detail is practical: captured Philistine animals provide food and possibly pack animals for the fugitive band. The victory at Keilah establishes David as a protector of Judahite towns even before he is their king.
6: When Abiathar fled to David at Keilah, he had the ephod with him. This retrospective note explains the oracle consultations of vv2 and 4: Abiathar's arrival with the ephod gave David access to the binary oracle mechanism that had been at Nob. The ephod (the high-priestly vestment housing the ûrîm wəṯummîm oracle stones) is now in David's camp — and absent from Saul's. This is a significant transfer of covenant-inquiry capacity.
7: Saul is told David has gone to Keilah and thinks: 'God has sold him into my hand, for he has shut himself in by entering a walled city with gates and bars.' Saul's theological interpretation — 'God has given him to me' — is a misreading of the situation. He assumes the walls that protect Keilah from Philistines will trap David for him. The irony is that Saul is reading providence exactly backwards: what YHWH has used to save Keilah, Saul thinks YHWH is using against David.
8: Saul summons all the people to war, to go down to Keilah to besiege David and his men. The king who just massacred YHWH's priests is now mobilizing the army to pursue YHWH's anointed. The trajectory of Saul's reign is now clear: the man appointed to protect Israel from its enemies is using Israel's military resources against the man YHWH has designated as his successor.
9: David knows that Saul is plotting evil against him and says to Abiathar the priest: 'Bring the ephod.' David's knowledge of Saul's planning is not specified — it may be intelligence reports or prophetic awareness — but his response is immediate consultation. The ephod is the proper instrument of decision; David does not act on information alone but seeks divine direction.
10: David prays: 'YHWH, God of Israel, your servant has heard that Saul seeks to come to Keilah, to destroy the city on my account. Will the leaders of Keilah give me up into his hand? Will Saul come down, as your servant has heard? YHWH God of Israel, please tell your servant.' The prayer frames two binary questions for oracle response: (1) Will the leaders hand David over? (2) Will Saul come? Each requires a yes/no answer from the ephod oracle.
11: YHWH answers: 'He will come down.' David presses further: 'Will the men of Keilah give me and my men over to Saul?' YHWH says: 'They will give you up.' The answer is uncomfortable: the very city David just saved will surrender him to his pursuer. This is not ingratitude so much as political reality — Keilah cannot withstand Saul's siege on David's behalf. YHWH's oracle is not encouraging; it is honest. David's response is immediate departure.
12: YHWH's answer that Keilah's leaders would surrender David confirms the necessity of flight. The detail is theologically important: David left Keilah not to save himself from betrayal by ungrateful citizens, but because YHWH told him to. Providence and oracle together direct his movements. He does not wait to see whether the prophecy proves true; the oracle is sufficient authorization for action.
13: David and his men — now about 600 — leave Keilah and wander wherever they can go. When Saul is told David has escaped from Keilah, he abandons the campaign. The force has grown from 400 (22:2) to 600 — the fugitive band is attracting more recruits even under pressure. 600 is a symbolically significant number in the Hebrew Bible (the size of Saul's fighting force in 13:15 and the original Danite migration in Judg 18:11).
14: David stays in the wilderness in strongholds and in the hill country of Ziph. Saul hunts for him every day, but God does not give David into his hand. The summary statement — 'God did not give him into his hand' (wəlōʾ-nəḵānô ʾĕlōhîm bəyāḏô) — is the theological verdict on the entire chase. Saul's persistent military resources are overridden by a simple divine restraint. The wilderness strongholds are not David's defense; YHWH's refusal is.
15: David fears because Saul has come out to seek his life. David is in the wilderness of Ziph at Horesh. The vulnerability is real — Saul's intelligence network is locating David despite the wilderness. Fear is the appropriate human response to a well-resourced enemy; it is also the context in which Jonathan's visit (v16) arrives as YHWH's answer.
16: Jonathan, Saul's son, rises and goes to David at Horesh and strengthens his hand in God (waḥazzeq ʾeṯ-yāḏô bēʾlōhîm). The phrase is theologically precise: Jonathan does not merely encourage David emotionally but strengthens his grip on God — reinforces his trust, his covenant relationship, his faith. This is the last recorded meeting between Jonathan and David. It is the covenant friend's final act before his death at Jezreel (31:2).
17: Jonathan says: 'Do not fear, for the hand of Saul my father shall not find you. You shall be king over Israel, and I shall be next to you.' Jonathan's confession — 'you shall be king' — is more than comforting prediction; it is covenant affirmation of David's divine appointment at cost to himself. Jonathan speaks of his own subordination ('I shall be next to you') without resentment. He has fully embraced that David's kingship displaces the inheritance he would have received as Saul's heir.
18: Both of them make a covenant before YHWH. Jonathan returns to his house and David remains at Horesh. The covenant renewal at Horesh is the third Jonathan-David covenant (first in 18:3; renewed in 20:16-17; now again here). Each covenant is more costly than the last: this one is made while David is a fugitive and Jonathan is the crown prince who should logically be David's enemy. The covenant's persistence through these conditions is its theological weight.
19: The Ziphites come up to Saul at Gibeah and betray David's location: 'Is David not hiding among us in the strongholds at Horesh, on the hill of Hachilah, which is south of Jeshimon?' The Ziphites are from Judah — they are betraying their own tribesman. The willingness of Judahites to hand David to Saul reflects the political complexity of tribal loyalty: Saul is the king, and the consequences of harboring the king's enemy are severe.
20: The Ziphites offer to deliver David to Saul whenever the king desires. Their initiative is pro-active treachery: they are not responding to a royal demand but volunteering information for political favor. Psalm 54 is headed 'When the Ziphites came and told Saul, David is hiding among us' — the psalm channels David's response to betrayal into worship.
21: Saul blesses them: 'May you be blessed by YHWH, for you have had compassion on me.' The irony is acute: Saul invokes YHWH's blessing on the men who are helping him hunt YHWH's anointed. The blessing-formula for informers against the anointed king is self-defeating — YHWH's blessing does not follow acts that oppose YHWH's purposes. Saul's religious language has been completely severed from YHWH's actual will.
22: Saul instructs the Ziphites to learn David's location with precision: 'Go, make yet more sure. Know and see the place where his foot is, and who has seen him there.' The intelligence operation Saul runs is detailed and methodical. He wants location, cover patterns, and eyewitness confirmation. His persistence contrasts with his earlier inability to sustain military initiatives (13:8-15; 15:9).
23: 'See and learn all the hiding places where he hides, and come back to me with sure information, and I will go with you. And if he is in the land, I will search him out among all the thousands of Judah.' Saul's comprehensive threat — he will search through all Judah's clans — reflects the totalizing logic of his paranoia. His kingdom's resources are now fully redirected toward eliminating a single man who has not raised his hand against Saul.
24: The Ziphites leave ahead of Saul. David and his men are in the wilderness of Maon, in the Arabah to the south of Jeshimon. The wilderness of Maon is in the southern Judean highlands south of Hebron — rugged, semi-arid terrain ideal for concealment. Saul and his men approach on one side of the mountain while David and his men are on the other.
25: David hears that Saul has come and goes down to the rock in the wilderness of Maon. Saul hears and pursues David into the wilderness of Maon. The mountain becomes a fulcrum: the pursued and the pursuer circling opposite sides, neither able to break through to the other. The geography creates a dramatic near-miss that the narrator prolongs deliberately.
26: Saul goes on one side of the mountain and David with his men on the other. David is hurrying to escape from Saul. Saul and his men were closing in on David and his men to seize them. The Hebrew here uses the verb yāʾaṭ (surround/encircle) — the tactical maneuver is an encirclement, not just a chase. The trap is almost complete.
27: A messenger comes to Saul: 'Hurry and come, for the Philistines have raided the land.' The providential timing is exact: the moment of maximum danger for David is the moment YHWH causes a Philistine raid to call Saul away. The external military threat that Israel's king is supposed to address breaks the encirclement. YHWH's rescue comes not through miracle but through the geopolitical pressures that routinely pull kings away from their obsessions.
28: Saul turns back from pursuing David and goes against the Philistines. This place was called the Rock of Escape (Selaʿ-hammaḥlĕqôṯ) — the Rock of Divisions, or the Rock of Escape — named after the event where the mountain divided pursuer from pursued. Place-name etiologies in Samuel mark the moments of YHWH's dramatic intervention with memorial geography.
29: David goes up from there and lives in the strongholds of En-gedi. En-gedi — the Spring of the Wild Goat — is a dramatic oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea, where fresh springs cascade through the rock faces of the Judean wilderness. The strongholds there are nearly inaccessible. David has moved from Adullam to Keilah to Ziph to Maon and now to En-gedi — a trajectory deeper into the wilderness as Saul's pressure increases.
